title: Secrets of the Lost Tomb - A Masterclass in Abundance - Part Four
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=eLhCLA0Jndo

The closing verdict on Daniel (@dungeondive)'s favourite game of all time. The faults are presentational — ugly art, amateurish graphic design — and Daniel argues he can overlook all of it because of what the game gets right.

Five reasons it sits at number one:

1. **Pure fun** — the most pure-fun dungeon crawl Daniel has played; a power-fantasy that never sacrifices tension or danger.
2. **Exploration** — the illusion that anything can happen at any time, but with random elements bound so tightly to the pulp theme that nothing reads as arbitrary. *Ninjas and lizard people while being visited by the Terminator* is on-genre, not off-tone.
3. **Bottomless-box-from-turn-one** — nothing is locked behind campaign progression. The super-weapon you'd normally earn at hour ten can come out of the search deck on turn one.
4. **Designer enthusiasm** — feels like the team were kids in a candy store, building a *big-budget indie game* with real respect for the pulp-adventure genre.
5. **Personal fit** — the game runs on the same fuel as the authors Daniel reads: A. Merritt, H. Rider Haggard, Lynn Carter, Robert E. Howard. Pulp without the racism, structurally.

Hope for the reprint: keep the messy core, leave the scenarios alone, hire a modern pulp illustrator and a better graphic designer. Don't repeat the *Lost Station* mistake of changing too much.

----

Daniel structures his all-time number-one around five specific things, not one. What's the board-game equivalent of an A. Merritt novel for you?
